texts.orig: Liquid  desiccant  air-conditioning  (LDAC)  system  is  one  of  the  excellent  alternatives  to  conventional  vapor compression  refrigeration  system  due  to  its  great  energy-saving  potential.  However,  regeneration  of  diluted liquid desiccant is the main energy-consuming process, and its performance plays a significant role in LDAC system.  Recently  vacuum  membrane  distillation  (VMD)  is  becoming  an  emerging  and  promising  separation process rely on the advantage of high permeate flux and low energy losses. The interest of usage solar energy techniques for feed solution heating in VMD systems is regarded as a sustainable path for membrane distillation process.  In  this  work,  a  solar-driven  VMD  system  incorporating  a  vapor-compression  heat  pump  for  liquid desiccant regeneration is introduced. An adapted solar/heat pump hybrid heating strategy has been devised to establish  a  connection  between  the  heat-demanding  process  and the  solar  thermal  supply. A  comprehensive multicriteria assessment of energy, exergy, and environmental evaluation is conducted, and the influences of crucial parameters on both key components and system performance are evaluated to ensure a systematic examination. Results show that both the COP and STEC decrease obviously with the growth of the inlet solution temperature, whereas SEEC is improved abviously from 410.6 kW ⋅ h/m 3 to 566.1 kW ⋅ h/m 3 due to the effect of heat pump. The exergy analysis indicates that exergy losses in solar collector and stratified heat storage tank accounted  for  over  80  %  of  the  total  exergy  losses.  Additionally,  single-objective  optimization  and  multiobjective  optimization  are  employed  to  explore  the  optimum  operating  conditions  of  key  componets  and whole system. Results show the ideal optimal solution can be obtained with a COP of 19.92 and a SEEC of 142.89 kW ⋅ h/m 3 when the weights of COP and SEEC are taken as [0.5, 0.5].

In LDAC systems, liquid desiccant absorbs moisture from humid air and become diluted along with air handling process. To maintain the dehumidification capacity of the desiccant, it needs to be concentrated and regenerated in a regenerator, in which the diluted solution is heated to a required temperature to achieve regeneration process. texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the energy quality of regeneration, the liquid desiccant regeneration systems could be classified as thermal-based regeneration, electrical-based  regeneration  and  hybrid  energy  driven  regeneration systems.  There  are  lots  of  studies  on  the  integration  of  membrane distillation technology with various heat sources, such as solar energy, heat pump, low-grade waste heat, and the use of heat recovery equipment to improve overall performance. Zhao et al [18] used solar energy and a diesel heater as the driving system for the vacuum-multi-effectmembrane-distillation (V-MEMD) module, and the solar-driven membrane system showed good performance at low operating temperatures (45 -60 ◦ C). In addition, the effects of heating, cooling, and feed conditions on module performance were studied, and the concept of VMD was combined with multiple-effect distillation to recover heat and improve energy efficiency. Naidu et al. [19] developed a single-stage V-MEMD system model, and analyzed the feasibility of high salinity water under different  operating  conditions (sustainable  low feed  temperature and flow rate of 1 -3 m/s), and evaluated the improved design of the highsalinity water VMD system. Shim et al. [20] added recirculation flow in the study of multi-stage VMD and achieved a water recovery rate of over 40 %. In addition, with the increase of recirculation flow ratio, the thermal  efficiency  also  improved,  confirming  the  great  influence  of recirculation flow on the concentration of the solution. Kim Y et al. [21] studied and developed a solar-assisted multi-stage vacuum membrane distillation system, in which a heat recovery unit was used to recover energy  from  permeate  steam  to  feed  seawater,  and  a  temperature modulation scheme was implemented to attenuate temperature fluctuations in feed seawater. Chen et al. [22] studied the vacuum multi-effect membrane  distillation  system  (MEMD)  used  for  regenerating  liquid desiccant, and validated the model with experimental data. It was found that  the  differences  in  regeneration  rate  and  saltwater  concentration were within 10 % and 5 %, respectively. With the developed and validated model, various MEMD configurations were studied, and the effects of key operating parameters were evaluated. Si et al.[23] proposed a vacuum membrane distillation system coupled with heat pump for the treatment of sulfuric acid solution which could achieve the evaporation process with lower temperature of 318.15 K-333.15 K. It was effective to reduce the specific heating energy consumption and elevate the coefficient of performance. Ma et al. [24] designed a small-scale desalination unit  for  producing  drinking  water  in  remote  places,  aiming  both  to recover latent heat of evaporation and to condense vapor without using an additional intensive cooling system. Results showed that for a tiny module (0.18 m 2 ),  a  daily freshwater production of 3.7 L can be obtained with an average electric consumption of 17 W (~0.13 m 2 PV).

texts.orig: Many previous studies have been conducted mostly on the integration of the liquid desiccant regeneration with solar energy or heat pump system  to  improve  the  overall  efficiency  of  LDAC  system.  [25 -28] However, the intermittent nature of solar energy impedes the sustainable application of solar assisted liquid desiccant system [29,30]. texts.orig: Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic diagram of the proposed solar assisted heat  pump-driven  vacuum  membrane  distillation  system  for  liquid desiccant regeneration, which is composed of a VMD regenerator, solar collector, heat pump, a stratified heat storage tank, heat recovery unit, vacuum pump, heat exchangers and other auxiliary components. In the proposed system, solar energy serves as the main heat source, which is collected  by  the  solar  collector  for  desiccant  solution  heating.  Heat pump is applied as an auxiliary heat source for the system when solar energy resources are scarce. The stratified heat storage tank is employed to store surplus heat from solar collector, overcoming the instability of solar energy. Moreover, the storage tank could also distribute heat energy and control the inlet solution temperature of the VMD regenerator. Depending on different solar radiation intensity, the proposed system can operate in the following two modes:
texts.text: Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic diagram of the proposed solar assisted heat  pump-driven  vacuum  membrane  distillation  system  for  liquid desiccant regeneration, which is composed of a VMD regenerator, solar collector, heat pump, a stratified heat storage tank, heat recovery unit, vacuum pump, heat exchangers and other auxiliary components. In the proposed system, solar energy serves as the main heat source, which is collected  by  the  solar  collector  for  desiccant  solution  heating.  Heat pump is applied as an auxiliary heat source for the system when solar energy resources are scarce. The stratified heat storage tank is employed to store surplus heat from solar collector, overcoming the instability of solar energy. Moreover, the storage tank could also distribute heat energy and control the inlet solution temperature of the VMD regenerator. Depending on different solar radiation intensity, the proposed system can operate in the following two modes:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (1) Mode 1: When the intensity of concentrated solar radiation is high enough to meet the heat requirement of regeneration, desiccant solution can be directly heated to the regenerate temperature, and the surplus heat energy from solar collector is stored in the stratified heat storage tank.

texts.orig: (2) Mode 2: When heat storage is exhausted in the absence of solar energy  resources,  the  heat  pump  is  activated  to  heating  the  liquid desiccant solution for regeneration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  heat  and  mass  transfer  process  during  VMD  regeneration  is illustrated in Fig. 2, which can be divided into three main classes: (1) Heat  is  transferred  from  the  dilute  solution  through  the  thermal boundary layer to the membrane surface; (2) Water evaporates from diluted solution, absorbing latent heat at the membrane surface, and the heat is transported to the permeate side through the membrane pores. Meanwhile, water molecule is transferred to the permeate side driven by the pressure difference between two sides of the membrane. (3) Heat is extracted alongside water vapor via a vacuum pump and subsequently transferred to other components.

Meanwhile, water molecule is transferred to the permeate side driven by the pressure difference between two sides of the membrane. (3) Heat is extracted alongside water vapor via a vacuum pump and subsequently transferred to other components.

texts.text: 3.1.4. Heat pump models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the intermittence and instability of solar energy, an auxiliary heating system by heat pump is conducted for solution heating when solar radiation intensity cannot meet the need of the solution regeneration. The heat pump subsystem model and the equation for calculating the heat transfer coefficient for R134a are shown in Appendix A and Appendix D, respectively.
texts.text: Due to the intermittence and instability of solar energy, an auxiliary heating system by heat pump is conducted for solution heating when solar radiation intensity cannot meet the need of the solution regeneration. The heat pump subsystem model and the equation for calculating the heat transfer coefficient for R134a are shown in Appendix A and Appendix D, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Variable frequency drive technology is adopted in heat pump subsystem, which adjusts the refrigerant flow by changing the compressor ' s rotational speed. It is achievable to regulate the heat supply from solar collector  or  the  stratified  heat  storage  tank  by  adjusting  operation pressure and working fluid flowrate of the compressor.
texts.text: Variable frequency drive technology is adopted in heat pump subsystem, which adjusts the refrigerant flow by changing the compressor ' s rotational speed. It is achievable to regulate the heat supply from solar collector  or  the  stratified  heat  storage  tank  by  adjusting  operation pressure and working fluid flowrate of the compressor.

texts.orig: increases, the membrane flux variation curve accelerates upwards, and the membrane flux at vacuum pressure of 4 kPa is higher than that at 10 kPa, which shows that there is a positive correlation between vacuum pressure and membrane flux. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the flow rate of the inlet  solution  directly  affects  the  outlet  solution  temperature  and membrane flux. With the increase of flow rate and Reynolds number of the solution, the heat transfer coefficient between the solution in the channel  and  the  membrane  surface  is  improved,  accelerating  the movement of water molecules towards the membrane pores. When the inlet flow rate is 80 L/h, the membrane flux reaches its maximum value of 6.226 kg/m 2 ⋅ h. At the same time, the increased flow velocity weakens the heat transfer intensity per unit mass of the solution, resulting in a downward-opening parabolic trend for the outlet temperature. However, the influence of solute concentration on membrane flux is more complex. An increase in solution concentration leads to a decrease in membrane flux following a downward trend, as shown in Fig. 6(c). The concentration change between the inlet and outlet solutions gradually decreases from 1.32 % to 0.83 %, indicating that the regeneration difficulty increases with the higher inlet solution concentration.

texts.orig: Solar collector is another major component in the proposed system, and its performance is greatly affected by the environmental parameters, including  solar  radiation  intensity,  dry  bulb  temperature  and  wind speed. In this study, outdoor environmental parameters in Beijing are taken as the typical calculated values, which are shown in Appendix C. It can  be  observed  from  Fig.  7(a)  that  the  utilized  solar  energy  and instantaneous  thermal  efficiency  of  the  collector  resonate  with  the fluctuations in solar radiation intensity throughout the 24-hour period. The solar radiation intensity, utilized solar energy and instantaneous heat  collecting  efficiency  reach  their  peak  simultaneously  at  around 12:30, which are 880 W/m 2 ⋅ K, 618 W/m 2 ⋅ K and 36.4 % respectively. Fig. 7(b) illustrates the waterfall chart of the solution temperature at each  time  in  different  nodes  of  the  stratified  heat  storage  tank.  The average temperatures of five representative layers are recorded, which are 89.34 ℃ , 88.86 ℃ , 88.10 ℃ , 86.75 ℃ and 77.90 ℃ from the top to bottom.

texts.orig: The effects of desiccant solution flow rate on the performance of the proposed system are illustrated in Fig. 9. The proposed system operates in Mode 2, in which both the solar collector and heat pump need to supply thermal energy for solution heating, as shown in Fig. 9(a). The figure indicates that, with the inlet solution flow rate increasing from 30 L/h to 80 L/h, the system STEC and SEEC show a synchronously increase with an almost parallel trend, while the COP slowly decreases from 6.15 to 3.98. The reason for this phenomenon can be analyzed from Fig. 9(b): the increase of flow rate reduces the heat transfer of the solution, which is mainly recovered in the plate heat exchanger and condenser. As the flow rate increases, the power consumption of the compressor in the heat pump system rises rapidly from 62.7 W to 473.4 W, while the power consumption of the solution pump and vacuum pump remains almost unchanged.  Moreover,  the  increase  in  solution  mass  flow  rate  can improve the heat transfer of the plate heat exchanger and condenser, as shown in Fig. 9(c). texts.orig: Fig. 10 shows the effects of solution concentration on the COP, STEC and SEEC of the system. The proposed system operates in Mode 2, in which both of the solar collector and heat pump need to supply thermal energy for solution heating, as shown in Fig. 10(a). It can be observed that the COP and STEC increase linearly with the growth of the inlet solution  concentration,  while  the  SEEC  rises  in  an  upward-opening parabolic trend. Specifically, with the increase of solution concentration from 5 wt% to 30 wt%, system COP and SEEC gradually increases from 5.04 and 415.3 kW ⋅ h/m 3 to 5.85 and 550.40 kW ⋅ h/m 3 , while the STEC boosts significantly from 4197.73 kW ⋅ h/m 3 to 5980.10 kW ⋅ h/m 3 . As shown in Fig. 10(b), with the inlet solution concentration increasing, both the power consumption of the vacuum pump and the compressor gradually decrease, leading to an increase in the system ' s COP and SEEC. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 10(c), as the increase of inlet solution concentration, heat transfer in condenser and plate heat exchanger is reduced because regeneration of liquid desiccant becomes more difficult and the water yield lessens. Typically, when the inlet solution concentration  is  30  wt%,  the  heat  transfer  in  condenser  and  plate  heat exchanger  is  reduced  by  0.25  kW  compared  with  the  inlet  solution concentration of 5 wt%.

texts.text: 4.1.3. Performance comparison with other systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  demonstrate  the  superiority  of  SAHP-VMD  system  for  liquid desiccant  regeneration,  a  performance  comparison  is  conducted  between the proposed system and vacuum multi-effect membrane distillation system from Ref.[22]. Fig. 13 presents the performance comparion between the proposed system and the reference system from literature [22]. It can be observed that the proposed system exhibits a certain superiority when feed solution concentration is over 25 wt%. However, V-MEMD system has a lower specific SEC when the feed solution concentration is below 25 wt% at the regeneration temperature of 70 ◦ C. This can be attributed to the unique design of V-MEMD, which efficiently  absorbs  an  amount  of  waste  heat  from  low  concentration solutions. Taking into account the potential applications of the THIC system,  it  becomes  evident  that  regenerating  solutions  with  higher concentrations  holds  greater  practical  benefits.  This  observation  underscores the significant potential of the proposed system for achieving energy savings.

texts.orig: In Fig. 14(a), over 80 % of total exergy destruction is attributed to the solar collector, stratified heat storage tank,  and  VMD  module. Conversely, the steam condenser, plate heat exchanger, and evaporator exhibit relatively low exergy destruction, accounting for as little as 3.2 %. This difference is due to the superior heat exchange efficiency of these components, maintaining exergy efficiency consistently above 50 %. However, the solar collector ' s  exergy  efficiency  is  relatively  low, ranging from 8 % to 11 % with increasing solution temperature. The exergy efficiency of the stratified heat storage tank gradually decreases from 60.7 % to 41.8 % due to the exergy destruction fluctuations when the solution temperature increases from 55 ◦ C to 80 ◦ C.

texts.orig: 4.2.2. The effects of solar radiation intensity on the exergy performance The  effect  of  solar  radiation  intensity  on  the  components  exergy destruction and exergy efficiency is shown in Fig. 15 (a) and 15 (b). It can be observed that in Mode 2, the exergy destruction of the VMD regenerator,  steam  condenser,  plate  heat  exchanger,  evaporator,  and condenser fluctuate within a very small range. The exergy destruction of the solar collector increases significantly from 0.172 kW to 0.730 kW as the solar radiation intensity increases, and the exergy destruction of the stratified heat storage tank increases slightly from 0.438 kW to 0.571 kW. In Mode 1, with the increase of soalr radiation the exergy destruction of solar collector continues to increase, and the exergy destruction of stratified heat storage tank firstly increases and then decreases, while the exergy destruction of condenser and evaporator is zero. The effect of solar radiation intensity on the energy efficiency is shown in Fig. 15(b), and significant  differences  between  the  two  operating  modes  can  be observed. The exergy efficiency of the solar collector increases gradually from 9.11 % to 11.53 %, and the exergy efficiency of the evaporator increases from 92.85 % to 97.46 %. The exergy efficiency of the stratified  heat  storage  tank  showes  a  trend  of  firstly  decreasing  and  then increasing, similar to the changes of exergy destruction in the stratified heat storage tank.

texts.orig: 4.3. System optimization
texts.text: 4.3. System optimization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to benefiting thermodynamic investigation, it is important to choose the appropriate operation conditions, providing efficient operation  efficiency.  Aforementioned  parameter  analysis  is  used  to study the effect of single parameter on system performance, and thus the optimum condition cannot be obtained. To optimize the key components and SAHP-VMD system, single objective and multi-objective optimization are introduced to comprehensively optimize operation parameters through the application of Genetic algorithm and NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II).
texts.text: In addition to benefiting thermodynamic investigation, it is important to choose the appropriate operation conditions, providing efficient operation  efficiency.  Aforementioned  parameter  analysis  is  used  to study the effect of single parameter on system performance, and thus the optimum condition cannot be obtained. To optimize the key components and SAHP-VMD system, single objective and multi-objective optimization are introduced to comprehensively optimize operation parameters through the application of Genetic algorithm and NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II).

texts.orig: 4.3.1. Single objective optimization of VMD regenerator
texts.text: 4.3.1. Single objective optimization of VMD regenerator

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to analyze the optimal thermodynamic performance of the VMD regenerator, a single objective genetic algorithm is used to optimize the performance of the component. Here, the number of evolutionary  generations  are  set  to  5000  and  other  genetic  algorithm parameters are shown in Table 5. In the assessment of membrane performance or membrane selection, the results of single-objective optimization specifically targeting membrane flux (N) will play a pivotal role. However, in assessing the energy utilization efficiency of the VMD regenerator  and  aiming  to  enhance  system  efficiency,  the  results  of single-objective optimization for GOR play a crucial role. Therefore, the membrane  flux  N  and  the  regenerative  thermal  efficiency  GOR  are chosen as the objective functions for this optimization. Besides, the inlet solution  temperature,  concentration,  flow  rate,  vacuum  degree  and membrane porosity are set as the decision variables in the optimization algorithm. The range of decision variables in the optimization process is shown in Table 6.

texts.orig: To further analyze the effect of constraint functions on the optimal values, the Pareto frontier is analyzed by using the TOPSIS (Technique for  Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution) method. The TOPSIS  method  is  an  effective  method  commonly  used  in  multiobjective decision analysis, first proposed by C.L. Hwang and K. Yoon [41] in 1981, which evaluates the relative merits of existing objects by sorting  the  proximity  of  a  finite  number  of  evaluation  objects  to  an idealised goal, with the aim of finding a ranking solution that approximates the ideal solution. In order to investigate the system performance under balanced and extreme scenarios, three typical weights [0.1,0.9], [0.5,  0.5]  and  [0.9,  0.1]  [42]  are  taken  for  the  two  optimization objective functions of N and GOR, respectively. The decision variables for the TOPSIS selected solver set are shown in Table 11, and its ideal point is shown in Fig. 19. The ideal points are identical for different weights, which in turn gives its ideal optimal solutions N as 15.65 kg/ m 2 ⋅ h and GOR is 84.87 %.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To reduce the energy consumption of the LDAC system and achieve efficient regeneration of the dilute solution, this paper conducted a solar assisted  heat  pump-driven  vacuum  membrane  distillation  system  for liquid  desiccant  regeneration.  Two  different  operation  modes  were introduced according to the intensity of solar radiation and the heatdemanding. Through the establishment and solution of the theoretical models, a parametric study was carried out to examine the performance of  the  proposed  system  from  the  respective  of  energy  and  exergy. Moreover, both single-objective optimization and multi-objective optimization of the system were conducted through the application of Genetic algorithm and NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II). The following are the conclusions:
texts.text: To reduce the energy consumption of the LDAC system and achieve efficient regeneration of the dilute solution, this paper conducted a solar assisted  heat  pump-driven  vacuum  membrane  distillation  system  for liquid  desiccant  regeneration.  Two  different  operation  modes  were introduced according to the intensity of solar radiation and the heatdemanding. Through the establishment and solution of the theoretical models, a parametric study was carried out to examine the performance of  the  proposed  system  from  the  respective  of  energy  and  exergy. Moreover, both single-objective optimization and multi-objective optimization of the system were conducted through the application of Genetic algorithm and NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II). The following are the conclusions:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1. The influences of diluted solution parameters and solar radiation intensity on key components and system performance are considered. Results  show  that  the  proposed  system  can  operate  using  only  solar energy as the heat source when the regenerated solution temperature is in the range of 55-62 ℃ , while both of the solar collector and heat pump need to supply thermal energy for solution heating if the solution inlet temperature  exceeds  62 ℃ .  Addtionnally,  both  the  COP  and  STEC decrease obviously with the growth of the inlet solution temperature, whereas  SEEC  is  improved  abviously  from  410.6  kW ⋅ h/m 3 to  566.1 kW ⋅ h/m 3 due to the effect of heat pump.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For steady-state analysis, solar radiation intensity of 800 W/m 2 , ambient air temperature of 10 ℃ and an air velocity of 6 m/s are chosen as the standard parameters. Fig. F.1 presents the solution temperature variations when it passes each component of the system. As can be seen in Fig. F.1(a), the dilute solution is firstly heated to 26.8 ℃ by the steam condenser, then enters the plate heat exchanger to absorb heat from the concentrated solution, accomplishing primary heat recovery and raising the temperature up to 48.6 ℃ . Subsequently, the dilute solution is pumped into the solar collector and is heated to 64.9 ◦ C. Considering the regeneration of the dilute solution at 70 ℃ , then the solution absorbs the condensation heat from the heat pump, achieving the fourth temperature increase to 76.5 ℃ in the condenser. Finally, the dilute solution enters the stratified heat storage tank to store the excess heat. Fig. F.1(b) illustrates the cooling process of the hot concentrated solution. After passing through the VMD, plate heat exchanger and heat pump, the concentrated solution is cooled down to 30.9 ◦ C and stored in the concentrated solution tank.
texts.text: For steady-state analysis, solar radiation intensity of 800 W/m 2 , ambient air temperature of 10 ℃ and an air velocity of 6 m/s are chosen as the standard parameters. Fig. F.1 presents the solution temperature variations when it passes each component of the system. As can be seen in Fig. F.1(a), the dilute solution is firstly heated to 26.8 ℃ by the steam condenser, then enters the plate heat exchanger to absorb heat from the concentrated solution, accomplishing primary heat recovery and raising the temperature up to 48.6 ℃ . Subsequently, the dilute solution is pumped into the solar collector and is heated to 64.9 ◦ C. Considering the regeneration of the dilute solution at 70 ℃ , then the solution absorbs the condensation heat from the heat pump, achieving the fourth temperature increase to 76.5 ℃ in the condenser. Finally, the dilute solution enters the stratified heat storage tank to store the excess heat. Fig. F.1(b) illustrates the cooling process of the hot concentrated solution. After passing through the VMD, plate heat exchanger and heat pump, the concentrated solution is cooled down to 30.9 ◦ C and stored in the concentrated solution tank.

texts.orig: Based on the above analysis, a system energy flow diagram (Sankey energy balance diagram[52]) is performed to describe the energy balance among components in the proposed system. The width of the branches in the energy flow diagram represents the amount of the energy, as shown in Fig. F.2. The solution passes by the VMD module, plate heat exchanger, and the evaporator of the heat pump before being stored in the concentrated solution tank, and the total heat recovery amounts to 1.42 kW during this process. Besides, the heat flow of the condensate water (including water vapor) from the vacuum side of the membrane distillation module enters the fresh water tank with a heat of 0.01 kW.
texts.text: Based on the above analysis, a system energy flow diagram (Sankey energy balance diagram[52]) is performed to describe the energy balance among components in the proposed system. The width of the branches in the energy flow diagram represents the amount of the energy, as shown in Fig. F.2. The solution passes by the VMD module, plate heat exchanger, and the evaporator of the heat pump before being stored in the concentrated solution tank, and the total heat recovery amounts to 1.42 kW during this process. Besides, the heat flow of the condensate water (including water vapor) from the vacuum side of the membrane distillation module enters the fresh water tank with a heat of 0.01 kW.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The exergy flow shown in Fig. F.3 can be divided into four types, including electrical exergy flow from the electric drive unit, exergy flow of the solution, exergy flow of the refrigerant, and exergy flow of the condensate water. The exergy flow of the hot solution at temperature of 70 ◦ C is 0.17 kW, and then drops down to 0.05 kW after completing the exergy recovery by the components at all levels. Correspondingly, the exergy flow of the dilute solution at temperature of 25 ◦ C is 0.04 kW, and increasingly reaches 0.14 kW by absorbing heat from the components at all levels, in accordance with the second law of thermodynamics. The exergy flow of the condensate water starts from the vacuum side of MD, and gradually releases 0.03 kW of energy flow during solution regeneration.
